did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models, specifically a hybrid transformer-CNN architecture named GlobalSkinNet for skin lesion classification and SkinFormNet (combining SegFormer and U-Net with attention mechanisms) for skin lesion segmentation. These AI methods were applied to dermoscopic images from benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020, HAM10000, etc.) to obtain classification and segmentation results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes novel deep learning models (GlobalSkinNet for classification and SkinFormNet for segmentation) and reports original experimental results, including specific accuracy and Dice coefficient metrics on multiple benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-2019, etc.). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is presenting new technical contributions and validation, not summarizing prior literature.

title: Advanced hybrid transformer CNN framework for improved skin lesion classification and segmentation
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel dual-architecture framework for skin lesion classification and segmentation to address challenges like non-homogeneous luminance and hair occlusion in dermoscopic images. The classification model, GlobalSkinNet, combines convolutional and transformer components using local attention with a global query, while the segmentation model, SkinFormNet, integrates SegFormer feature extraction with a U-Net architecture augmented by attention mechanisms. GlobalSkinNet was evaluated on the PH2, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020, and HAM10000 datasets, achieving accuracies of 100%, 98%, 97%, and 98%, respectively. SkinFormNet was assessed on five benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-2016, ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and HAM10000), yielding Dice coefficients ranging from 0.94 to 0.99. The results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid transformer-CNN framework effectively improves both classification and segmentation performance compared to existing methods.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, etc.) and binary labels (malignant vs. benign).
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes GlobalSkinNet for skin lesion classification. It predicts discrete diagnostic categories on datasets like PH2, ISIC-2019, and HAM101000 (multiclass) and binary malignant vs. benign labels on ISIC-2020. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and confusion matrices.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise segmentation masks identifying the boundaries of skin lesions within dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes SkinFormNet for skin lesion segmentation. It generates segmentation masks for lesion areas. Performance is measured using the Dice coefficient on datasets including PH2, ISIC-2016, ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and HAM10000.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic skin cancer dataset comprising 200 high-quality images obtained under controlled conditions with minimal acquisition artifacts and consistent lighting. It primarily comprises images from a European population.

use.training: Used for classification (GlobalSkinNet) and segmentation (SkinFormNet). Split details not explicitly reported in text, though Table 4 indicates 'Random' split.
use.validation: Used for classification and segmentation. Split details not explicitly reported in text.
use.testing: Used for classification and segmentation. Split details not explicitly reported in text.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A repository of 10015 dermoscopic images at resolutions around 600x450, captured under different lighting conditions and with varying color calibration. It represents heterogeneity from actual clinical practice with images acquired from multiple institutions.

use.training: Used for classification (GlobalSkinNet) and segmentation (SkinFormNet). Split details not explicitly reported in text, though Table 4 indicates 'Random' split.
use.validation: Used for classification and segmentation. Split details not explicitly reported in text.
use.testing: Used for classification and segmentation. Split details not explicitly reported in text.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2016
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising 1279 images of malignant and benign masks, developed from multiple sources within the ISIC archive. It exhibits considerable heterogeneity in resolution, inconsistent quality, and low-contrast images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 2750 skin images including ground masks, introduced to handle limitations of ISIC-2016 in quality standardization and size. It contains clinical variations from real-world settings, predominantly with lighter skin tones.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising 3694 images with 5 ground truth masks each, across different resolutions and various lighting conditions. It largely overlaps with the HAM10000 dataset and includes seven diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising 33569 images with eight diagnostic categories. It includes data from different continents, with balanced gender representation and a wider age distribution, and limited skin types typically I-III.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising 44108 images from dermoscopic and clinical sources, shifting the task focus to a binary-class problem (malignant vs benign). It expands the problem scope beyond conventional dermoscopy to develop multimodal solutions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance and stability of the GlobalSkinNet model on the ISIC-2020 dataset.
To visualize and analyze the classification outcomes, including true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each dataset.
To assess the diagnostic ability of the GlobalSkinNet model across different classification thresholds for each dataset.
To validate the model's balanced performance across all lesion types and handle class imbalance, particularly in datasets like HAM10000 and ISIC-2019.
To evaluate the segmentation performance of the SkinFormNet model on benchmark datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA with p-values) was reported. The paper utilized bootstrap analysis with 1000 samples to estimate 95% confidence intervals for classification metrics on the ISIC-2020 dataset to demonstrate statistical reproducibility and robustness, but did not perform significance tests comparing models.

ISIC-2019.notes: GlobalSkinNet achieved 98% accuracy. Comparison with Yang et al. [38] (90.7%), Ayas et al. [21] (97.2%), and Ahmad et al. [17] (97.7%).

ISIC-2020.notes: GlobalSkinNet achieved 97% accuracy. Bootstrap analysis with 1000 samples showed 95% confidence intervals for accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Comparison with Wan et al. [25] (95.6%), Ahmad et al. [17] (96.9%), and Ju et al. [27] (90.2%).

architecture_used.pretraining: SkinFormNet uses a pre-trained SegFormer model for feature extraction. GlobalSkinNet was trained from scratch.

components: PatchEmbed layer (3x3 convolution, stride 2, padding, producing overlapping 16x16 patches)
Alternating Local Window-based Self-Attention (LWSA) and Global Context Self-Attention (GCSA)
Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) with relative positional embeddings
MLP with Dense1 (RD -> 2048), ReLU, Dense2 (2048 -> RD), and skip connections
GlobalSkinNet.heads: Multi-Head Attention (number of heads not explicitly specified, but dimension per head dk=D/H is defined)
GlobalSkinNet.classifier_head: LayerNorm -> Global Average Pooling -> Dense -> ReLU -> Dropout -> Dense -> SoftMax

components: U-Net architecture augmented with attention layers
Attention layers comprising a selected subset of neurons with activation units

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (SoftMax activation implies Cross-Entropy for classification)
training_details.training_schedule: Trained from scratch for GlobalSkinNet; SegFormer pre-trained for SkinFormNet

classification_or_output_head.GlobalSkinNet: Dense layer with SoftMax activation for multi-class classification

preprocessing_techniques: Patch extraction via PatchEmbed layer (3x3 convolution with stride 2 and padding to produce overlapping 16x16 patches)
Data augmentation using vertical and horizontal flips

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as data augmentation during training for the GlobalSkinNet and SkinFormNet models.

conclusions: The proposed GlobalSkinNet classification model achieved accuracies of 100%, 98%, 97%, and 98% on the PH2, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020, and HAM10000 datasets, respectively.
The proposed SkinFormNet segmentation model achieved Dice coefficients of 0.97, 0.98, 0.94, 0.99, and 0.96 on the PH2, ISIC-2016, ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and HAM10000 datasets, respectively.

GlobalSkinNet effectively handles class imbalance, as evidenced by macro-averaged precision and recall metrics remaining nearly identical for rare classes (e.g., VASC in HAM10000 and NV in ISIC-2019).
The model demonstrates reliable identification of melanoma (MEL), with sensitivity ranging from 0.9225 to 1.0000 across datasets.
Bootstrap analysis on the ISIC-2020 dataset confirmed the statistical reproducibility and robustness of the classification results.
The hybrid transformer-CNN framework addresses challenges such as non-homogeneous luminance, low contrast, and hair occlusion in dermoscopic images.
The study acknowledges that fair-skinned populations are more vulnerable to skin cancer and that current datasets may have limitations regarding skin type diversity (typically types I-III).
The authors note that new adaptive models are needed to support continuous learning and adapt to new subclasses without catastrophic forgetting, highlighting a limitation of static models.
important_remarks: The manuscript is an unedited 'Article in Press' version; content may contain errors and is subject to change before final publication.
The PH2 dataset consists primarily of images from a European population with sparse demographic information, limiting generalizability.
The HAM10000 and ISIC datasets exhibit significant skin type bias, predominantly representing Fitzpatrick skin types I-III with limited representation of types IV-VI.

The study acknowledges that current static models do not account for continuous data collection in clinical settings and highlights the need for adaptive models to handle new subclasses without catastrophic forgetting.
The ISIC-2019 dataset has limited skin types (typically I-III) despite broader demographic representation in gender and age.
The ISIC-2016 dataset exhibits considerable heterogeneity in resolution, inconsistent quality, and low-contrast images, which may challenge model robustness.

The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for statistical significance between models, relying instead on bootstrap analysis for confidence intervals on the ISIC-2020 dataset.
